name: powerconf
documentation:
# powerconf

Powerful configuration tools for numerical simulation.

`powerconf` allows you to write configuration files for things like physics simulations
with support for variable interpolation and expression evaluation. Consider a simulation
that will solve some partial differential equation on a 2-dimensional Cartesian grid. Perhaps
the simulation itself requires us to set the min and max range and the number of points
to use along each axis. A simple YAML configuration for the simulation might look something
like this

```yaml
grid:
    x:
        min: 0 cm
        max: 1.5 cm
        N: 151
    y:
        min: 0 cm
        max: 1.0 cm
        N: 101
```
This is fine, but it might be useful to specify the resolution to use instead of the number of points.
With `powerconf`, we can write a configuration file that looks like this

```yaml
grid:
    resolution: 1 um
    x:
        min: 0 cm
        max: 1.5 cm
        N: $( (${max} - ${min})/${../resolution} + 1)
    y:
        min: 0 cm
        max: 1.0 cm
        N: $( (${max} - ${min})/${../resolution} + 1)
```
In this example, we give a resolution to use for both x and y directions and then calculate the number
of points to use with an expression. Note the relative paths to configuration parameters used in the
expressions. `powerconf` uses the [`fspathtree`](https://github.com/CD3/fspathtree) module to provide
filesystem-like access to elements in a nested dict.


## Install

Install with pip

```bash
$ pip install powerconf
```

functions.name: powerconf.graphs.get_dependency_chains
documentation: Given a node, return the chain of nodes that depend on it. If multiple paths exists, each is returned. If node does not have any predecessors, an empty list is returned.

functions.name: powerconf.utils.get_id
documentation:
Return a unique id for the given configuration object.

@param path_predicate: a function that will be passed the path to each node
in the tree and should return true if the path sould be included in the
tree when creating the id. This is useful for removing config parameters
that are expected to change, but don't chnge the actual configuration.

functions.name: powerconf.utils.apply_transform

modules.name: powerconf.loaders
documentation: A collection of functions for loading config trees from a file or string.

functions.name: powerconf.loaders.yaml
documentation: Load a configuration tree from a string or file.

functions.name: powerconf.loaders.yaml_all_docs
documentation: Load all documents in YAML file.

functions.name: powerconf.yaml.powerload
documentation:
Load a set of configurations from a YAML files.

If the file contains multiple documents, the first document is assumed to be
a base configuration with all following documents being partial configs that are applied
on top of the base configuration. This makes it easy define multiple configuration
that only differ by a few settings in a single file.

If configuration tree includes '@batch' nodes, these will be expanded into multiple configurations.

For each configuration that is generated after considering all YAML documents and expanding all
batch parameters, expressions are evaluated. This may include variable references to other configuration
parameter.

This is your one-stop-shop for loading powerconfigs from YAML files.

classes.name: powerconf.expressions.ExpressionEvaluator
documentation: A class for evaluting expressions in a separate environement.

classes.name: powerconf.expressions.ExecExpressionEvaluator
documentation: A class for evaluating expressions using the builtin exec(). BE CAREFUL!

functions.name: powerconf.cli.load_config
documentation: load config from a file.

functions.name: powerconf.cli.main
documentation:
The `powerconf` command is a CLI for the powerconf python module. It allows you to read
a configuration file, evaluate all expression, expand all batch nodes, etc, and write
the "rendered" configurations to a file(s).

functions.name: powerconf.cli.version
documentation: Print the version number.

functions.name: powerconf.cli.render
documentation:
Read a powerconf-enabled configuration file and write a rendered configuration file.

The rendered file(s) will be generated by rendering the template with each
configuration instance as a context.

functions.name: powerconf.cli.print_instances
documentation: Print instances of configuration trees generated from the config.

functions.name: powerconf.rendering.expand_partial_configs
documentation:
Give a list of configuration trees, treat the frist tree
as a "base" configuration and all other trees as partial configurations
that should be merged with the base config to create instances.

@param include_base : if true, the base config will be included in the returned set.

functions.name: powerconf.rendering.try_construct_quantity
documentation:
Try to construct a pint.UnitRegistry().Quantity
object from a given object, if the given looks like it would be
a quantity.

By default, an object is considered a quantity if it:
    - is a string,
    - begins with a numerical value,
    - does not throw an exception when passed to pint.UnitRegistry().Quantity(...) constructor

@param is_quantity: a function returning true if the object should be considered a quantity. This allows the caller to provide
                    their logic to identify quantities.
@param quantity_class : the class that should be used to construct quantities. pint cannot handle quantities from different
                        registries, so this allows the caller to provide the class they are uing.

functions.name: powerconf.rendering.expand_variables
documentation:
Expand shell-style variables into python variables

@param template: optional template string used for variable expantion. The variables
                 name will be inserted into the template using {VARNAME}. Default value
                 is "ctx['{name}']", which means we will have:

                $x -> c['x']
                ${x} -> c['x']
                $/grid/x -> c['/grid/x']
                ${/grid/x} -> c['/grid/x']

functions.name: powerconf.rendering.contains_expression
documentation:
Returns true if a string contains expression.
Supports passing not strings as arguments, in which
case False is returned.

functions.name: powerconf.rendering.contains_variable
documentation:
Returns true if a string contains a variable refernce.
Supports passing not strings as arguments, in which
case False is returned.

functions.name: powerconf.rendering.load_includes
powerconf.rendering.render_mustache_template
documentation:
Render a Mustache template text string using the values in a context. The context can
be a configuration tree that has been previously loaded and rendered with powerconf.

methods.name: powerconf.rendering.ConfigRenderer.expand_and_render
documentation: Expand batch configurations and render each instance.

methods.name: powerconf.rendering.ConfigRenderer.expand_batch_nodes
documentation: Expand @batch nodes in a configuration tree into multiple configuration trees.

methods.name: powerconf.rendering.ConfigRenderer.render
documentation: Render a configuration tree, constructing all quantities, expanding all variables, and evaluating all expressions.

methods.name: powerconf.rendering.ConfigRenderer._evaluate_all_expressions
documentation: Evaluate the expressions in a configuration tree, using a graph of the tree dependencies to determine the render order.

methods.name: powerconf.rendering.ConfigRenderer._evaluate_expressions
documentation: Evaluate expression in the config at the paths listed. Uses as a utility function on _evaluate_all_expressions(...)

methods.name: powerconf.rendering.ConfigRenderer._construct_all_quantities
documentation: Replace strings in the tree representing quantities with pint.Quantity objects.

methods.name: powerconf.rendering.ConfigRenderer._expand_all_variables
documentation: Expand all shell-style variables into python variables in the entire tree.

methods.name: powerconf.rendering.ConfigRenderer._get_batch_leaves
documentation:
Return a list of keys in a fpathtree (nested dict/list) that are marked
as batch.
